summary:
The agent acknowledges that while flexible instruments are effective for navigating tight spaces like those found in urological procedures due to their articulating capabilities, they have not been studied or demonstrated in the context of creating proper femoral tunnels during ACL reconstruction at 90 degrees of knee flexion. Therefore, based on current available information, flexibility of instrumentation does not provide sufficient support to confirm whether these tools achieve adequate tunnel length under this specific surgical condition.

Okay, I need to summarize Agent1's reasoning strictly according to the requirements — only two sentences focusing solely on clinically relevant points related to the original question about flexible instruments forming femoral tunnels at 90-degree knee flexion in ACL surgery.

First sentence should establish what the agent knows regarding use cases of flexible instruments – but must tie them back explicitly to orthopedic joint work rather than general medical uses. Second sentence needs to state clearly why we lack confidence in applying this technology here despite its theoretical advantages.

I'll avoid mentioning unrelated fields entirely since even brief references might distract; instead focus exclusively on how instrument design properties relate to actual outcomes needed in ACL graft placement. Must emphasize both technical mismatch between soft-tissue navigation vs bone drilling mechanics AND evidentiary gap showing no studies validate success rates.
